slides.description: The slide is titled "AI Screenplays" in large blue text at the top. The main image depicts a detailed scene from the animated TV show "South Park," set within a well-appointed office overlooking the Hollywood sign at sunset. A character resembling a studio executive, dressed in a black jacket and red tie, stands next to the cardboard robot "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" (Awesom-O), which is prominently featured. The office includes a large wooden desk adorned with golden award statues, including Oscar-like and Emmy-like trophies, and a red collectible figure of Cartman. On the wall to the right, a framed picture shows "AWESOM-O" with a red stamp reading "LAME." Documents resembling screenplays are visible on the desk, alongside a rotary phone and a framed family photo. This introductory slide visually establishes the topic of AI in screenwriting using a recognizable, satirical pop culture reference to Awesom-O, a character known for generating uninspired movie ideas.
slides.narration: Hey everybody, I'm Taylor, your instructor, and uh, welcome to 'AI Screenplays'! You know, when we talk about AI and creativity, a lot of folks immediately think of Awesom-O here, right? Just, like, spitting out generic, kinda lame movie ideas. But today, we're gonna dive way deeper than that, what the hell. We're gonna talk about building *agentic flows* to generate truly complex, long-form content – specifically, screenplays! This isn't just about movies, though; it's a super powerful architecture for *any* structured long document, okay? Let's get into it!

slides.description: This slide is titled "Generating Long Documents" in large blue text at the top. It features four bullet points outlining the core concept and objective. The first bullet states that "AI can be used to generate long, coherent text documents," followed by the second bullet listing examples such as "Books, reports, screenplays." The third bullet point specifies the lecture's focus: "Today we will build an agentic flow to generate screenplays for movies." The final bullet indicates the broader applicability of the method: "This flow architecture can be generalized to other long text documents." This slide transitions from the satirical introduction of AI screenplays on the previous slide to a more formal overview of the capabilities of AI in generating extended text and outlines the practical goal of the lecture.
slides.narration: Okay, so, like, that Awesom-O stuff was kinda funny, right? But seriously, AI *can* be used to generate really long, coherent text documents, you know? I mean, we're talking full-on books, detailed reports, and yeah, screenplays! Today, our main mission, our goal, is to actually build one of these agentic flows specifically for movie screenplays. But the cool thing is, this architecture, this whole system we're gonna build, it's super generalizable. You can use it for all sorts of other long text documents too. Awesome, right?

slides.description: This slide is titled "Challenge: 'One-Shot' Long-Form" in large blue text, immediately following the introduction of AI's ability to generate long documents. It highlights the primary difficulty an AI faces in generating a long, coherent document in a single attempt. The slide explains this challenge through the concept of "local vs global" coherence, noting that AI excels at writing small parts but struggles to maintain overall consistency across an entire document. Specific issues like the AI becoming "lost in the middle" and experiencing "context drift" are mentioned, where the AI can hallucinate or lose focus deep into the text. This slide effectively pivots from the potential of AI in long text generation to the practical limitations that necessitate a more sophisticated approach, implicitly setting up the need for the "agentic flow" mentioned on Slide 2.
slides.narration: Okay, so we just talked about how awesome it is that AI *can* generate these long, coherent documents, right? But here's the kicker, the big challenge: trying to do it all in a 'one-shot' go? Like, generating an entire screenplay from start to finish with just one prompt? Uh, that's where it really falls apart, folks. The AI is fantastic at writing small, *local* parts – a sentence, a paragraph, no problem. But maintaining *global* coherence across a whole, complex document? That's super difficult. It just gets 'lost in the middle,' you know? There's massive context drift where, deep into the document, the AI can start to hallucinate or completely lose focus of what the hell it was even writing about. This is why we need a better approach, right?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Movie Screenplays" in large blue text. It presents two main bullet points detailing the complexity of screenplays. The first bullet explains that "Screenplays have many different elements the AI needs to capture," listing "Story shape," "Goals of 'sequences' in the movie," and "Scene action and dialogue" as examples. The second main bullet, "Hierarchical structure of a screenplay," breaks down a screenplay's organization into progressively detailed levels: "Premise – basic elements of the movie," "Story arc – high level plot structure," "Sequences – more detailed plot," and "Scenes – even more detailed plot." This slide elaborates on the challenge of generating long-form content, specifically screenplays, by illustrating their inherent structural complexity, which was implied as a difficulty for AI in the previous slide's discussion of 'one-shot' generation and context drift.
slides.narration: So, we just talked about how 'one-shot' generation for long documents like screenplays just doesn't work, right? And this slide really hammers home *why* that is. I mean, screenplays, they've got so many damn moving parts, you know? The AI needs to capture the overall story shape, the goals of different 'sequences' within the movie, and then all the specific scene actions and dialogue. But the *real* kicker is their hierarchical structure. It's not just one big blob of text. You start with a high-level Premise, then you build out the Story arc, which breaks into Sequences, and *then* you get to the individual Scenes. That's a lot of layers, you people! So, trying to generate all that at once? Uh, yeah, that's a recipe for disaster. This complexity is exactly what we need to tackle, right?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Screenplay Flow" in large blue text at the top. It features a flowchart diagram that visually outlines an agentic process for generating screenplays, building upon the hierarchical structure introduced in the previous slide. The flow begins with a blue rectangular node labeled "Premise" with "premise.json" underneath, leading via an arrow to a purple rectangular node labeled "Arc" with "arc.json." From the "Arc" node, two arrows diverge: one points to an orange rectangular node labeled "Sequences" with "sequence.json," and the other points downwards to a green rectangular node labeled "Scenes" with "scenes.json." Each node includes a small icon resembling a brown box with eyes, possibly indicating an agent or data processing step. This diagram concretely illustrates the 'agentic flow' architecture mentioned earlier, demonstrating how AI can generate long documents by breaking down the complex hierarchical structure of a screenplay into sequential, manageable steps to address 'one-shot' generation challenges.
slides.narration: Okay, so, we just talked about how screenplays are super complex, right? All those damn layers from premise to scenes? And how trying to do it all at once with one-shot AI is just, like, impossible? Well, boom! This, folks, this is our solution. This is the 'Screenplay Flow' diagram. You see how we're breaking it down? We start with a high-level Premise, which gives us our `premise.json`. Then that feeds into the Arc, which generates `arc.json`. And from there, we build out our Sequences, getting `sequence.json`, and finally, all the individual Scenes in `scenes.json`. This hierarchical, agentic approach, uh, this is how we tackle that complexity and build a coherent, long-form document. Awesome, right?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Premise Agent" in large blue text and details the function of the first agent in the screenplay generation flow. It describes a chat agent that discusses the core concept of a movie with the user, covering elements like title, logline, characters, genre, and central conflict. The agent can provide plot ideas if the user is stuck, and the conversational process is noted to take a few minutes to solidify all major elements. This agent's output is a "premise.json" file, directly corresponding to the initial 'Premise' node in the flowchart from Slide 5. A small image of the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot is present in the top right corner, continuing the visual motif introduced in Slide 1.
slides.narration: Okay, so, now we're diving into the first actual agent in our flow, right? This is the Premise Agent. And it's a chat agent, you know? Like, you literally just talk to it. You're gonna discuss what your movie is all about. We're talking title, the logline – which is, like, that one-sentence executive summary, right? – characters, genre, the central conflict. And if you're stuck, uh, you can even ask it for plot ideas. It's awesome! This whole chat, I mean, it can take a few minutes. You want to make sure you solidify all those major elements. And then, boom, it spits out our `premise.json` file. That's our first step in building this thing.

slides.description: The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large blue text, directly displaying the output of the 'Premise Agent' discussed in the previous slide. The main content is a code block showing a JSON file named 'premise.json', which encapsulates the core concept of a movie. Key fields visible include a 'project_id', 'title' ("Shadow of the Dark World"), 'writers' (listed as "TBD"), and 'format_notes' indicating "Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat." The JSON also contains a detailed 'logline' describing a quest involving characters reminiscent of 'The Legend of Zelda', along with 'genre' (fantasy, action, adventure) and 'themes' (hero's journey, destiny, etc.). This slide provides a tangible example of the structured data generated during the initial premise-setting step of the agentic screenplay flow, illustrating how the foundational elements of a movie are captured before further development.
slides.narration: Alright, so after you chat with that Premise Agent, right? Boom! This is what it spits out, our `premise.json` file. You know? Look at this! We've got our `project_id`, the `title` – 'Shadow of the Dark World,' awesome! – and right there, that `logline` describing our hero, Link, going on a quest after his uncle is wounded. You can see the `genre` too: fantasy, action, adventure. And the `themes` like 'hero's journey' and 'destiny.' This, folks, is the foundation, our first solid piece of data for the whole damn screenplay. Right?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large blue text, continuing the display of the JSON output from the 'Premise Agent' discussed in earlier slides. The main content is a code block showcasing further details of the 'premise.json' file, which outlines the foundational elements of a movie concept. This section of the JSON explicitly details the "central_conflict" for the movie, describing Link's quest to stop Aghanim and Ganon from destroying Hyrule. It also elaborates on the movie's "setting," including the "primary_time" as a high fantasy era and listing specific "primary_places" like Hyrule Castle and Kakariko Village. Furthermore, the JSON presents details for the "protagonists" array, clearly defining 'Link' with his role as an "unassuming hero" and his primary 'want.' This slide serves to further illustrate the granular detail captured by the initial agent in the screenplay generation process.
slides.narration: Okay, so, sticking with our `premise.json` here, right? We're diving even deeper into the juicy bits. Look, you've got the `central_conflict` laid out – Link's whole damn race against time to stop Aghanim and Ganon from trashing Hyrule and, uh, the Light World. And right below that, the `setting`! We've got 'High fantasy era' – which is kinda cool, right? – and all the key places like Hyrule Castle, Kakariko Village. Boom! And then, look, our `protagonists` array, kicking off with Link, detailing his role as an 'unassuming hero' and his main 'want' to save everyone. I mean, this is seriously detailed stuff, you know?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large blue text, continuing the display of the foundational movie concept from the 'Premise Agent.' It showcases further details within the JSON code block, specifically focusing on the main characters and the overarching stakes of the story. The JSON defines the 'protagonists' array, clearly listing Link with his role as an "unassuming hero" and his primary 'want' to save Hyrule. It then details the 'key_antagonist' as Aghanim, describing his role as Ganon's pawn and his goal to release Ganon. A 'key_ally,' Sahasrahla, is also defined with his role as a guide and his objective to help Link acquire the Master Sword. Finally, the 'stakes' for the movie are explicitly stated as "The total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon." This slide completes the comprehensive overview of the 'premise.json' file, illustrating how all critical high-level story elements are established before moving to subsequent stages of the screenplay generation flow.
slides.narration: Okay, so we're still deep in this `premise.json` file, right? We just looked at Link, our unassuming hero, and his want to save Hyrule. Now, we've got our `key_antagonist` clearly defined as Aghanim. He's Ganon's pawn, you know, and his whole damn goal is to seal the maidens and release Ganon. Boom. But, thankfully, we also have a `key_ally`, Sahasrahla, the wise man. He's gonna guide Link to get the Master Sword and stop Aghanim's plans. And then, folks, look at the `stakes`! It's the total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon. I mean, that's what we're fighting for, what the hell! This `premise.json` is pretty solid, right? All the major pieces, the foundation, is here. Awesome.

slides.description: The slide is titled "Arc Agent" in large blue text at the top, featuring the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot icon in the top right corner. This slide describes the next agent in the screenplay generation process, following the Premise Agent. It details a chat agent that discusses the movie's story arc, which consists of multiple acts. The agent explicitly takes the `premise.json` file as input for context, and users guide it by telling it what happens in each act, prompting it to draft them one at a time. The agent then creates an `arc.json` file. This slide elaborates on the 'Arc' stage from the 'Screenplay Flow' diagram (Slide 5), building directly upon the foundational `premise.json` content established in the preceding slides.
slides.narration: Okay, so we've got our super solid `premise.json` now, right? All the foundational stuff – conflict, characters, stakes – everything's there. Boom! Now, what's next in our 'Screenplay Flow' diagram? It's the Arc Agent! And just like the Premise Agent, this is another chat agent. You know? You're gonna talk to it about the *story arc* of your movie, which, uh, consists of multiple acts. The cool thing is, it takes our `premise.json` as input. So it's not starting from scratch, right? It's got all that context. You'll just tell it what happens in each act, then ask it to draft them, one at a time. And then, uh, boom, it creates our `arc.json` file. Awesome.

slides.description: The slide is titled "Arc.json" in large blue text, directly showcasing the output of the 'Arc Agent' introduced in the previous slide. The main content is a code block displaying a JSON file, specifically detailing 'Act 3' of the movie's story arc. The JSON includes a 'label' for this act ("Battle Aghanim and the Dark World"), an 'approx_pages_note' indicating it will be 20-30 pages of a 120-page screenplay, and a 'dramatic_question' outlining the core conflict. A comprehensive 'summary' details the plot points of Act 3, including Link's quest to defeat Aghanim and prevent Ganon's rise. It also features a 'turning_point_end' description and a list of 'must_hit_beats' for the act. This slide provides a tangible example of how the high-level story premise is developed into a structured, act-by-act narrative with detailed plot points, fulfilling the 'Arc' stage of the agentic screenplay generation flow.
slides.narration: Okay, so we've just fed our `premise.json` into the Arc Agent, right? And boom! This is what it spits out, folks: our `arc.json` file. And specifically, we're looking at `Act 3` here, you know? Look at the detail! We've got the `label` – 'Battle Aghanim and the Dark World,' pretty epic! And an `approx_pages_note`, telling us it's gonna be 20-30 pages of a 120-page screenplay, awesome. Then, uh, the `dramatic_question`: 'Can Link defeat Aghanim...?' Right? And a full `summary` of everything that happens in Act 3, all the way to Ganon's ultimate rise. We even have the `turning_point_end` and a list of specific `must_hit_beats` for the act. I mean, this is seriously breaking down the story, giving us a robust blueprint for the next stages, you people! What the hell, this is good stuff, right?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Sequence Agent" in large blue text at the top, continuing the visual motif with the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot icon in the top right corner. This slide describes the next agent in the screenplay generation flow, distinguishing it as an "automated agent (no chat)" responsible for writing the movie sequences for each act. It specifies that the agent's inputs include both the 'Premise.json' and 'Arc.json' files for global context, along with all previously generated sequences for local context. The primary elements it focuses on for each sequence are its narrative goal, key locations, and the 'exit beat' for transition. This agent ultimately creates a 'sequence.json' file, directly corresponding to the 'Sequences' node in the 'Screenplay Flow' diagram (Slide 5) and building upon the outputs of the previous Premise and Arc agents.
slides.narration: Alright, so we've got our `arc.json` now, right? We've broken down the whole damn movie into acts and their major beats. Boom! What's next in our flow? It's the Sequence Agent. Now, this one's different, folks. Uh, it's an *automated* agent, you know? No more chatting! It just takes all that info we've already generated – the `premise.json` and the `arc.json` – for that global view of the movie. And then, uh, it also takes all the *previous* sequences it's already written for local context. It focuses on the goal of each sequence, where it's happening, and, like, its exit beat, you know? And then, boom, it spits out our `sequence.json` file. Awesome.

slides.description: The slide is titled "Sequence.json" in large blue text, directly displaying the output of the 'Sequence Agent' introduced in the previous slide. The main content is a code block showcasing a JSON file, specifically detailing the first sequence of the movie. This 'sequence.json' includes a 'project_id' and 'sequence_count', and defines the initial sequence as 'SEQ1' with the title "Zelda's Urgent Plea." It specifies this sequence belongs to 'act' 1 and outlines its 'goal' to establish Link's call to adventure, his motivation, and to equip him for his journey. The JSON also details the 'emotional_tone' as "Urgent, sorrowful, determined," lists 'key_locations' like "Link's Home" and "Hyrule Castle (Outer Walls)," and provides an 'exit_beat' describing Link's resolve to infiltrate Hyrule Castle. This slide tangibly illustrates how the high-level story arc is broken down into detailed, actionable sequences, fulfilling the 'Sequences' stage of the agentic screenplay generation flow.
slides.narration: Okay, so we've got the Sequence Agent running, right? And boom! This, folks, is what it spits out: our `sequence.json` file. You know? Look at this detail for `SEQ1`, our *first* sequence. It's titled 'Zelda's Urgent Plea,' which, uh, sets the mood, right? It clearly states it's part of `Act 1`. And check out that `goal` – to establish Link's call to adventure, his motivation, and to get him his first tools. Awesome! You can see the `emotional_tone` is 'Urgent, sorrowful, determined' – intense, right? And the `key_locations` like 'Link's Home' and 'Hyrule Castle.' But the best part, that `exit_beat`? Link steels himself, ready to infiltrate Hyrule Castle. I mean, this is seriously breaking down the story into actionable parts, folks. What the hell, this is good stuff, right? This is how we build it.

slides.description: The slide is titled "Scene Agent" in large blue text, continuing the series describing the components of the agentic screenplay generation flow, with the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot icon in the top right. This slide describes the final agent, an "automated agent (no chat)" responsible for writing the individual scenes within each sequence. It specifies that this agent takes multiple inputs for context: `Premise.json`, `Arc.json`, and `Sequence.json` for a global understanding of the movie, plus all previously generated scenes within the current and prior sequences for local context. The primary elements the agent focuses on are the "Slug line" (location of the scene) and "Blocks" (actions and dialogues). Ultimately, this agent creates a `scenes.json` file, representing the most granular level of the screenplay, and directly corresponds to the 'Scenes' node in the overall 'Screenplay Flow' diagram (Slide 5), building upon all prior stages.
slides.narration: Okay, so we've broken down our whole damn movie into sequences with the Sequence Agent, right? Boom! What's the final piece of the puzzle in our 'Screenplay Flow'? It's the Scene Agent! This, folks, is where the magic happens at the granular level. And, like, good news: it's another *automated* agent, you know? No more chatting! It's gonna write all the individual scenes for each sequence. It takes all the context we've built so far: `premise.json`, `arc.json`, and `sequence.json` for that global view, right? Plus, uh, all the scenes it's already generated for local context. It focuses on the 'Slug line' – where the hell we are – and the 'Blocks' of action and dialogue. And then, boom, it creates our `scenes.json` file. This is how we get the real, usable screenplay!

slides.description: The slide is titled "Scenes.json" in large blue text, directly displaying the output of the 'Scene Agent' introduced previously. The main content is a code block showcasing the initial portion of a JSON file, specifically detailing the first scene of the movie. This 'scenes.json' defines a scene identified as 'S0001', with the slugline 'INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT' and belonging to 'SEQ1'. The JSON structure includes an array of 'blocks', which detail the scene's actions and dialogue. It describes Link waking up to a desperate, telepathic whisper from Zelda, who is trapped in Hyrule Castle by Aghanim. This slide provides a tangible example of the granular level of a generated screenplay, fulfilling the 'Scenes' stage of the agentic flow by illustrating how the high-level narrative is translated into specific actions and dialogue.
slides.narration: Okay, so we've run our Scene Agent, right? And boom! This, folks, is what it spits out: our `scenes.json` file. This is the real granular detail, you know? Look at this first scene, `S0001`. We've got the `slugline` right there: 'INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT,' awesome! And then, like, all the `blocks` of action and dialogue. You can see Link sleeping, then, uh, suddenly, a desperate telepathic whisper from Zelda, trapped by Aghanim. Boom! Link jolts awake, right? This is how our high-level narrative gets turned into actual, playable screenplay content. What the hell, this is good stuff, you people!

slides.description: The slide is titled "Enhancement Agents" in large blue text, introducing a new type of agent within the screenplay generation process. It features a main bullet point explaining that the agentic flow can be improved by using agents to enhance intermediate outputs, followed by sub-bullets listing examples such as writing better sequences or scenes, cleaning up the premise, and making the arc more creative. In the top right, a gold-colored robot icon labeled "ENHANCEMENT AGENT" is displayed, continuing the visual motif. At the bottom, the "Screenplay Flow" diagram from Slide 5 is reproduced, showing the connections between "Premise," "Arc," "Sequences," and "Scenes." Crucially, small robot icons, representing these enhancement agents, are now positioned on the arrows between these nodes, visually indicating their role in refining the outputs (e.g., premise.json, arc.json) before they are passed to subsequent stages. This slide proposes a mechanism to elevate the quality and consistency of the screenplay by refining the content generated by the primary agents discussed in previous slides.
slides.narration: Okay, so we've built the core flow, right? We've got our `premise.json`, `arc.json`, `sequence.json`, and `scenes.json`. But what if we want to make them *better*, you know? What if we want to add some real creative spice to those outputs? Boom! That's where these "Enhancement Agents" come in. You see them on the diagram, like, on the arrows between the stages? These aren't about generating the *next* step; they're about *refining* the *current* step. So, you can write better sequences, clean up that premise if it's a bit rough, or make your arc more creative. What the hell, this is how we take it to the next level, folks!

slides.description: The slide is titled "Generalizing to Other Documents" in large blue text. A single bullet point states that "By modifying the agent prompts, this hierarchical flow can be used for other long text documents." Below this, a table is displayed with columns titled "Document," "Premise," "Arc," "Sequence," "Scene," and "Artist Rendering." The table illustrates how the hierarchical structure previously applied to screenplays can be adapted for five different document types: Book (with 'Premise', 'Table of contents', 'Chapter', 'Sub-chapter'), Podcast (with 'Concept', 'Flow, format', 'Segment', 'Beat'), Song (with 'Premise', 'Structure (verse, hooks, coda)', 'Verse', 'Couplet'), Lecture (with 'Learning objective', 'Outline', 'Module', 'Slide'), and Business plan (with 'Value prop', 'Outline', 'Section', 'Sub-section'). Each row also features a small image in the 'Artist Rendering' column, often depicting the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot in a relevant context for that document type. This slide directly builds upon the 'agentic flow' and hierarchical breakdown introduced for screenplays, demonstrating the broader applicability of the method, as was initially hinted at in Slide 2.
slides.narration: Okay, so we just talked about how we can make our screenplays *better* with those Enhancement Agents, right? But the truly awesome thing about this whole architecture, folks, is that it's not just for screenplays! Boom! Look at this. By simply modifying the *prompts* for each of our agents, you can use this exact same hierarchical flow for, like, *any* long text document. You know? I mean, you want to write a book? Your Premise becomes your Book Premise, your Arc is your Table of Contents, Sequences are Chapters, and Scenes are Sub-chapters. What the hell! Or a podcast? Concept, flow, segment, beat. Or even a whole damn lecture, like this one! Learning objective, outline, module, slide. Right? This is how you take this powerful concept and apply it everywhere. Awesome!

slides.description: The slide is titled "Coding Session" in large blue text, indicating a practical, hands-on segment of the lecture. It presents three bullet points outlining tasks or objectives for the session: "Write a short screenplay using Awesome-O," "Add enhancement agents to the agentic flow," and "Modify Awesome-O for different document types." Below these points, the "Screenplay Flow" diagram from Slide 5 is reproduced, illustrating the connections between "Premise," "Arc," "Sequences," and "Scenes." Each node features a brown box-with-eyes icon, and significantly, small robot icons representing "Enhancement Agents" (introduced in Slide 16) are now depicted on the arrows connecting these nodes. This slide marks a transition from theoretical explanation to practical application, challenging the audience to implement the hierarchical agentic flow for screenplay generation and its generalizations, leveraging the concepts of core agents and enhancement agents discussed in preceding slides.
slides.narration: Alright folks, so we've explored the architecture, right? We've seen how to make it better with enhancement agents, and we've talked about how to generalize it for pretty much *any* damn long document. What the hell! Now, it's time to roll up our sleeves. We're gonna dive into a coding session! You people are gonna get to write a short screenplay using our Awesome-O agentic flow. We'll add some of those awesome enhancement agents to really spice things up. And guess what? You'll even get to modify Awesome-O to generate completely different document types! Boom. This is where you actually build and customize your own agentic flows. Let's do this!
